name: Joey Cantillo
reasoning: No injury or workload concerns surfaced — Cantillo (8-5, 3.74 ERA) is lined up on his normal turn at Tropicana Field with no mention of a shortened leash. Beat coverage notes he 'has recorded at least seven strikeouts in five of his past six starts' and, more specifically for tonight's road context, 'seven or more strikeouts in each of his last four road appearances,' which aligns with and even slightly exceeds the model's trailing K/9 marks of 13.8 (L3) and 12.5 (L30). On the opponent side, the Rays' own team strikeout tendency this season ranks 1st in the league in strikeouts against (730), suggesting this lineup is more whiff-prone than the generic 24.5% opponent-K-rate-vs-hand figure implies, even though Tampa's offense overall has been red-hot and owns the best record in the AL. Given the confirmed road-start K trend and the Rays' league-leading strikeout total working in Cantillo's favor, I'd nudge slightly above the stats-only model's 6.3 projection rather than below it.

name: Roki Sasaki
reasoning: Sasaki's last outing vs. the Yankees was encouraging on the swing-and-miss side (5 K over 5.2 IP, zero earned runs, touching 101.8 mph), which supports the trailing 8.0 K/9 mark and suggests his stuff is trending up, not down. However, Covers' matchup analysis flags that he's been in 'horrible form' of late, with a 7.28 FIP and 44.2% hard-hit rate over his last four starts, and he's still averaging only about 4.9-5.7 IP per start, capping total strikeout opportunities even when whiffs are strong. The Mets have been a hot-hitting, high-contact team lately (28 extra-base hits and a 40.5% hard-hit rate over their last 5-10 games), which could shorten his outing further via damage/pitch-count rather than punch-outs, so I land just under the model's 4.1 baseline-adjustment, slightly above it given the velocity/whiff uptick but not enough to clear the 4.5 line with confidence.
